item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I'm novoting this. I didn't feel the pacing, the description of him as a skip doesn't do much for me because none of his augmentations really come up in detail later so they're just little tidbits of information (not sure what a "better" way to do the description would look like, just saying how I feel), and the ending seemed anticlimactic, and not in a way that felt intentional. Or maybe it was intentional, and it just didn't land for me. Anyways, it's a novote instead of a downvote because the dialogue was still very well-written, and I, as always, enjoy reading Vincent Anderson for the sake of reading Vincent Anderson, it just didn't feel like this came together. I didn't feel an arc here. I didn't feel much progression. Didn't click. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's room in my heart for a lot of people, but the ones who exploit others for profit aren't among them.

I feel as though there is a very strong qualitative difference between "hast thou considered the financier" and "this broken person has destroyed everything in the pursuit of a profit they can no longer enjoy." I actually feel relatively little sympathy for Vincent Anderson, but still feel like this article has a great deal of merit.

Regardless of if you think that his late-life mea culpa is because he realized the hollowness of achievements and sought to achieve some measure of redemption, or because he no longer has cash resources to assuage the huge amounts of guilt he correctly feels, I think that both fit neatly within his characterization. After all, this is the man who interrupted his daughter's honeymoon to let her know that he was very sorry and that he was ever so generously giving her millions of dollars like a good, heroic person would. The fact that he contains such complexity doesn't serve to make him any less of a despicable person, or his actions any less heinous; giving someone depth and motivation doesn't, or at least shouldn't, excuse their actions.

Anderson chose to forsake literally everything in the name of profit and his company. He doesn't even have that anymore. I think that this is a lot richer than "the robot billionaire is sad :("

Eyyyyy.

Glad to see ol' Vincent Anderson himself finally crossing the century mark.

item.title: Re: Brilliant and poignant
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed the article.

item.title: Brilliant and poignant
item.content:encoded: I came to this SCP with no prior knowledge of Anderson Robotics, and it still seriously touched me. When I saw the concept, a corporate leader with cybernetic enhancements, I expected him to be dehumanized and vilified beyond belief, but instead, he's more human than many people at the Foundation. This skip speaks to the fact that everyone, no matter what they are or what they've done, has real emotions, motivations, and feelings. Even though he's basically a supervillain, he's extremely sympathetic, and my heart can't help but bleed for him. Take the easiest +1 I've ever given.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the same objection I had to filling out the Little Misters list, but people did that, too, so what I say doesn't matter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, having Anderson be an article doesn't make it any less of a GoI than Prometheus Labs, which famously blew itself up. Additionally, having this article here doesn't end the Anderson story at all, as the tale The Vanishing of Vincent Anderson proves, which takes place after the events of this article.

Downvote if you must, but I don't believe "effectively putting an end to Anderson Robotics as a GoI and story" is a tangible fear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dislike the idea of having Anderson be an article, a known quantity, and effectively putting an end to Anderson Robotics as a GoI and story. More than 60 people disagree with me, so my downvote doesn't mean much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Roget. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure how I missed this when it came out, I'm a big fan of Anderson and seeing him in this format is really cool. It's not always easy to make that jump from tale character to being an SCP article but this pulls it off well. Good characterization as usual, well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not like there isn't a link to an entire tale explaining what that means.

:V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3860 was initially apprehended by operatives of MTF Gamma-13 ("Asimov's Lawbringers") on 15/11/2018, during a sting operation within the Three Portlands Location of Interest5. Unbeknownst to SCP Foundation operatives, SCP-3860 avoided full apprehension until the joint UIU/Foundation raid on the Anderson Robotics Headquarters on 24/5/2024.

What? He was caught, but not really, and you don't bother to explain what that means?

Sorry, but that second sentence is just so annoyingly unhelpful. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A skip version of a captured Herman Fuller, you say? I've been binge-reading the whole Dread & Circuses hub lately, and that was one of the recent highlights. Much like Anderson here, this puts him in Foundation custody with severely curtailed abilities, but still leaves the door open for future (and past) narrative development.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But, here is the thing, we have articles that clash with the creation of headcanon all the time.

Various articles portray the SCPF as using anomalies to contain things and having Thaumatologists on payroll, while others have them poking all things anomalous away with a 10ft pole. We have some articles that show the Foundation squeaking by on a shoestring budget, and others that show them literally having all the money. So saying that an article limits the creation of headcanon is a very flawed measuring stick, in my opinion.

Additionally, unlike Herman Fuller, or Dr. Wondertainment, in which we see multiple interpretations of the characters and various story arcs, Anderson Robotics has had a major centralized story since before it even became a official GoI. Anyone whose given AR more than a cursory glance is likely to know this, and the capture of Vincent Anderson, is likely to be nothing new.

If it doesn't work for you it doesn't work for you, but downvoting as a means of defending the ability of others to form headcanons is not a service you will find a lot of need for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not saying that it clashes much with headcanon but limits the creation of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
¯\_(ツ)_/¯

If it clashes with your headcanon, it clashes with your headcanon.

Skip versions of a captured Herman Fuller and Doctor Wondertainment would be pretty cool though, if they were done with the proper execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love how it's written but I have mix feelings of having Anderson being an actual SCP article. It kinda irks me since it is like having Dr. Wondertainment or Herman Fuller themselves as SCPs. It ruins any sense of mystery behind the character to see them so crushed and nothing. I know there is no canon but the existence of this article and it's many cross links forces a reader into a rabbit hole, and limit their interpretations and personal continuity. Not to mention that they likely would have to feel obligated to follow it. Also, I personally find it better to have expanded material be done second than have an SCP based on heavy amounts of expanded material to avoid overwhelming readers.

Still, I feel folks outside the wiki view the mainlist as canon since what the article says the SCP does or is is canon (if it jumps then it jumps) and a tale can further expand it to add something new (now it talks). Now people must view the tales alluded to as canon to understand since it's built on it than the other way. I am not saying that it's a bad thing but it has too much significance to the narrative of the piece.

Overall, it's relies on established knowledge of other works and doesn't stand on its own while limiting headcanons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My work here is done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddammit Conwell, you made me give a damn about him. Goddammit.

+1 you wondrous monster

item.title: Some cool art.
item.content:encoded: Zhange done did make some spectacular art to accompany this article. Check it out (as well as the rest of her pieces) HERE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too sad to be rad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The logical step from sad robots being sad is to sad cyborg sadness.

+Sad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the kind words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn was I excited about this draft when I read it.

Emphatic +1, and christ, is this great.

item.title: Re: Hmmmmmmm
item.content:encoded: Ah. Well there you go!

item.title: Re: Hmmmmmmm
item.content:encoded: Actually, dude, I read a few more tales and scps with Anderson dialogue and I think my problem was more of an initial reaction to him saying things like "as long as the Robo-wizard…" and whatnot. I feel more comfortable with his writing now

item.title: Re: Hmmmmmmm
item.content:encoded:
Anderson speaks in a manner which seems to be much younger than him.

Could you elaborate on this? What it is that makes him sound like someone much younger than him?

If its a manner of him speaking like someone alive today despite being an old man, I would like to point out that all the dates place these interviews around a decade or more from now, so he'd have those kind of mannerisms. Especially if he doesn't really interact with too many people, since the vast majority of that time is spent in solitary confinement.

item.title: Hmmmmmmm
item.content:encoded: There are some problems I have with this SCP, particularly with the interviews. Anderson speaks in a manner which seems to be much younger than him. On dialogue writing, I am not convinced of Mr Anderson's old age. However, the rest of this SCP is well written I greatly appreciate you making Mr Anderson an SCP, I feel it's much better than just leaving him free and having a conflict that is never resolved (seemingly taking away the passage of time.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this would be better — passable, even — if I could feel anything for Anderson, but I don't. There's room in my heart for a lot of people, but the ones who exploit others for profit aren't among them.

Really, our culture has enough stories humanizing captains of industry, and this doesn't bring much of anything to the table.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now now, no tears. If history has shown anything, there is always one more Anderson arc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I found AR, I immediately fell in love with the masterful writing and the nuanced and sympathetic character of Anderson. I’m… both glad that this poignant epilogue came to be and deeply saddened at the fact that this is the end of Anderson as we know it. Words cannot fully encapsulate my feelings at seeing the great thaumaturge withering away for the remainder of his short life, deprived of his empire and drowning in regrets. Take your goddamned +1 and let me cry for a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fitting addition to a series of articles and tales that have been about increasingly sadder robots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God he's just so sad at this point. He's like that first stanza from Viva La Vida.

I used to rule the world

Seas would rise when I gave the word

Now in the morning I sleep alone

Sweep the streets I used to own

There's a really dark melancholy here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Testing for mass audience appeal, etc.

All of them really helped to polish this up, and I am grateful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit that is a lot of beta readers, kudos

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like we now see the face of the king of a kingdom.

also why is it not noted that the anomalous properties include being part of stories that are PRIME CRY MATERIAL WHY

+1 Conwell, what a way to start the new year.

item.title: Here we are.
item.content:encoded:
Well, here we are. Vincent Anderson, the man himself, is now a skip. For those of you just joining us, I hope that the footnote summaries and the like make the background to this character clear enough that the dialogue and character work has been enjoyable. For those of you who have been on the ride awhile, thanks for all the support on the way.

Thank you to all who reviewed this as a draft:
Modern_Erasmus, Decibelles, Taffeta, DrBleep, djkaktus, The Great Hippo, Randomini, Zyn, fieldstone, Sly161, Mr Carbon, taylor_itkin, DarkStuff, SunnyClockwork, minmin, BananaRepublic, 9Volt, MaliceAforethought, Metaphysician, Tufto, DrMagnus, ProcyonLotor, and A Random Day.

Extra special thank you to Shaggydredlocks for providing the initial suggestion on tackling this article.

All that being said, be sure to also check out SunnyClockworks new tale, which was also posted today: Anderson the Alternative.

Enjoy the skip.

JC
